Description: Do brisk walking, jogging, cycling, swimming, or similar moderate-to-vigorous movement for 20–45 minutes on most days.
Train major muscle groups 2–3 times per week with progressive overload and enough recovery.
Use a structured worksheet to log a situation, the emotion it triggered, the automatic thought, the evidence for and against it, and a balanced alternative.
See how a Belief (B) — not the Activating event (A) itself — drives your emotional Consequence (C), then dispute that belief to change the reaction.
Catch a distorted or exaggerated thought, weigh the actual evidence for and against it, and reframe it in more accurate, balanced terms.
Schedule small rewarding or valued actions before motivation appears, then track mood afterward.
Build a fear hierarchy and repeatedly face feared situations without escaping or using safety behaviors, advancing as each becomes manageable.
Trigger the obsession or fear while deliberately not doing the compulsion, reassurance, avoidance, or escape behavior.
Define the problem, generate options, choose one, act, and review the result.
Postpone worries to a scheduled 10–20 minute window and redirect attention when they arise outside it.
Tense and release each major muscle group in sequence while noticing the contrast.
Focus on breath, body, or sound, and gently return attention whenever the mind wanders.
Limit time in bed to actual sleep time, then gradually expand the window as sleep efficiency improves.
Write a specific 'If situation X happens, then I will do behavior Y' plan.
Set a hard but reachable target and compare progress against it regularly.
Record the target behavior or outcome daily so patterns become visible.
Review tracked data, identify the next bottleneck, and change one variable at a time.
Lock in money, reputation, or access ahead of time so that not following through on the desired action carries a real cost.
Repeat one small behavior after the same cue in the same context until it becomes automatic.
Attach a new behavior immediately after an existing reliable routine.
Make the good option the default and add extra steps to the bad option.
Draw on others for emotional, practical, and informational support — through close relationships or a group pursuing the same goal.
Slow down, attend to pleasant details, and deliberately extend the positive experience.
Spend regular time walking, sitting, or exercising in green or blue spaces.
Pick a realistic daily step target, track it, and increase it gradually when it becomes easy.
Breathe low into the belly at roughly 5–6 breaths per minute for several minutes.
Specify exactly when, where, how, and with what materials you will act.
Pair a beneficial task with an indulgence you only allow during that task.
Shrink the behavior until it feels easy, perform it after a cue, and briefly celebrate completion.
Put every open loop into one trusted inbox, clarify the next action, and review it regularly.
Change your surroundings so the desired behavior is easy and the undesired behavior is inconvenient.
Work out what you most deeply value in life, then use those values to decide which goals are worth setting and pursuing.
Step back and relate to thoughts as passing mental events rather than literal truths or commands — using techniques like labeling, repeating, or otherwise creating distance from them.
Write three specific things you feel grateful for each day.
Each evening, list three good events from the day and your role in them (or why they happened).
Before a specific task or performance, vividly rehearse yourself carrying out each step and technique well — the execution itself, not just the end result.
Describe the facts, express your feeling, specify the request, and state the consequences — in calm, direct language.
Reflect the other person's point and emotion before offering advice or disagreement.
Get bright light soon after waking — outdoors when possible, or from a light box — to anchor your circadian rhythm.
At day's end, note what happened, what worked, what failed, and tomorrow's priority.
Draft a short statement of principles, roles, and long-term aims and revisit it regularly.
Rate major life domains, choose the lowest rated high-value domain, and set one action.
Schedule distraction-free blocks for cognitively demanding work and remove communication channels.
Use bed only for sleep and sex, leave if awake too long, and keep a consistent wake time.
Wake at the same time every day, including weekends, to stabilize the sleep-wake rhythm.
Convert a vague aim into a Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time-bound target.
Name the Wish, Outcome, Obstacle, and Plan an if-then response to the obstacle.
Work on one task for about 25 minutes, take a short break, and repeat.
Reserve calendar blocks in advance for specific work you want to do during them, for time to rest, for time to exercise, etc.
Tell someone the exact commitment and report completion on a fixed schedule.
Notice suffering, remind yourself it is human, and speak to yourself as a good coach would.
Perform small concrete helpful acts and notice their effects on others and yourself.
Privately write for 15–20 minutes about a stressful event and what it means.
Write vividly about a realistic future in which important goals have gone well.
Identify a character strength and use it in a new way each day.
Write a brief uncensored account of thoughts, decisions, and lessons each day.
Do at least one tiny action toward the goal every day, even on bad days.
Expose yourself briefly to cold water, starting mild and short, and observe stress or mood effects.
Follow a fixed pattern such as box breathing, 4-7-8 breathing, or cyclic hyperventilation with recovery.
Notice craving sensations as waves, breathe, and wait without acting until they crest and fall.
Treat each small behavior as evidence for the identity you want to strengthen.
Pick one habit likely to improve other behaviors or make other habits easier and work on developing that habit first.
Mark each day you complete the habit and try to preserve the streak.
Do the most aversive high-value task first in the workday.
Take a planned break from compulsive high-reward behaviors and replace them with low-stimulation activities.
Stand or sit expansively for a minute or two before a challenging interaction.
While naming a distressing issue, tap through the standard point sequence and re-rate emotional intensity.
Create a collage of goal-related images or words.
Repeat short positive statements about yourself (e.g., 'I am strong and capable.').
Repeat that things tend to work out for you and look for opportunities that match the expectation.
Write a future scene as if the desired outcome has already happened.
Listen to two slightly different tones through headphones to try to induce a target mental state.
Play cognitive games daily, but expect improvement mainly on trained or nearby tasks rather than broad intelligence.
Accumulate practice hours, but do not expect hours alone to overcome feedback quality, genetics, timing, and domain constraints.
Split sleep into many short naps to reduce total sleep.
Write a desired outcome 3 times in the morning, 6 times midday, and 9 times at night.
Play affirming messages below conscious audibility while relaxing or sleeping.
Play tracks marketed as specific healing frequencies during meditation, rest, or work.
Hold or display a crystal to balance energy or heal yourself.
Choose goals or timing based on horoscopes, signs, or transits.
Select goals, dates, or habits based on personally meaningful numbers.
Think, visualize, or 'vibrate' as if the desired outcome is inevitable or has already happened.
Rapidly switch between tasks or media streams to get multiple things done at once.
Use juices, teas, fasting, or colon cleanses to 'flush toxins' from the body.
